romania.title: Asociația Tășuleasa Social inaugurează primul „Modul de găzduire pe Via  Transilvanica” la Șapartoc, în județul Mureș
romania.summary: Asociația Tășuleasa Social continuă dezvoltarea infrastructurii celui mai mare traseu de lungă distanță din România prin inaugurarea oficială a primului modul de găzduire Via Transilvanica. Situat în satul Șapartoc din… Articolul Asociația Tășuleasa Social inaugurează primul „Modul de găzduire pe Via Transilvanica” la Șapartoc, în județul Mureș apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Scena Sports Festival 2026 aduce sport și entertainment la un loc –  clase de sport, paradă, campioni și concerte cu Minelli, Loredana, Dirty Nano și MIRA
romania.summary: Scena Sports Festival vine anul acesta cu un nou program care reunește sportul și entertainmentul. Clase de mișcare, demonstrații, momente pentru copii, parada cluburilor sportive, un moment aniversar dedicat unor… Articolul Scena Sports Festival 2026 aduce sport și entertainment la un loc – clase de sport, paradă, campioni și concerte cu Minelli, Loredana, Dirty Nano și MIRA apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Simplitate și accesibilitate: Peste un an de cercetare și dezvoltare distilată într-un sistem „plug & play” disponibil 24/7. Într-o piață saturată de informații a căror veridicitate este greu de filtrat, platforma… Articolul Inner-maps lansează primul spațiu digital pre-terapeutic: platforma care traduce teoriile psihologice în „radiografii vizuale” pentru clarificare emoțională apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Munții Apuseni vor găzdui în 6 iunie 2026 ediția aniversară a msg Maraton Apuseni, unul dintre cele mai importante evenimente de alergare montană din România. Ajuns la a 15-a ediție,… Articolul Clujul devine, pentru al 15-lea an consecutiv, o referință pentru comunitatea de trail running din România și Europa de Est apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: APCAO lansează primul ghid de recuperare pentru copiii cu afecțiuni hematologice maligne, cu sprijinul Kaizen Foundation
romania.summary: APCAO (Asociația Părinților Copiilor cu Afecțiuni Oncologice) anunță lansarea oficială a ghidului multidisciplinar „Recuperarea Medicală a Copiilor cu Afecțiuni Hematologice Maligne”, o resursă de referință pentru îngrijirea oncologică pediatrică din România, dezvoltată cu sprijinul Kaizen Foundation.

romania.title: Când stadionul se golește, rămâne familia. Cristi Chivu și fetele lui, Natalia și Anastasia
romania.summary: „Dedic trofeul fiicelor mele, care au fost nevoite să suporte multe lucruri, mai ales la începutul sezonului, când tatăl lor era pus la îndoială”, a spus Cristi Chivu în 13 mai 2026, când a câștigat Cupa Italiei, al doilea trofeu din 2026. Cu doar câteva zile în urmă, echipa pe care o antrenează, Internazionale Milano, …

romania.title: Intervenţie pentru salvarea a doi adolescenți căzuţi într-un lac din Chitila. Au fost scoși din apă și transportați la spital
romania.summary: Pompierii, scafandrii și echipajele medicale au intervenit, joi după-amiază, pentru salvarea a doi copii care au ajuns în lacul Chitila, din județul Ilfov. Un băiat de 16 ani a fost resuscitat timp de aproximativ 30 de minute, a răspuns manevrelor salvatorilor și a fost transportat ulterior cu elicopterul SMURD la spital. Cea de-a doua victimă, o fată scoasă din apă înainte de sosirea echipajelor de intervenție, a primit primele îngrijiri la fața locului și a fost dusă, la rândul ei, la spital.

romania.title: Subiecte Evaluarea Națională 2026, clasa a II-a: Elevii au susținut proba la Matematică și Explorarea mediului. Când se anunță notele
romania.summary: Elevii de clasa a II-a au susținut astăzi, 14 mai, proba scrisă la Matematică și Explorarea mediului, în cadrul Evaluării Naționale 2026, după ce marți au avut testarea scrisă la Limba română, iar miercuri, 13 mai, au fost evaluați la competențele de citire.

Potrivit mai multor surse oficiale, liderii ţărilor participante la reuniunea B9 s-au distrat de minune aseară, până pe la 4 dimineaţa, când au semnat Declaraţia comună „Ş-altă dată, ş-altă dată o s-o facem şi mai, şi mai lată” şi s-au retras în camerele lor. romania.summary: Un studiu antropologic a scos la iveală că în cele două zile în care un băiețel de 5 ani dintr-un sat sibian a stat singur în pădure, 174.000 de bucureșteni s-au plâns pe social media că n-au destulă căldură sau apă caldă. „În locul acelor smiorcăiți nu aș mai ieși din casă o lună”, a […] The post Un băiețel de 5 ani a stat 2 nopți în pădure, timp în care mii de bucureșteni s-au plâns că n-au căldură appeared first on Times New Roman .

În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Hidden genetic defect linked to Kaposi sarcoma for the first time
medical.summary: A new study has uncovered a genetic cause that may explain why some people develop Kaposi sarcoma despite having no apparent immune deficiency. The rare cancer, which forms in the cells lining blood vessels, is caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8), also known as Kaposi sarcoma–associated herpesvirus.

medical.title: Not just an energy drink: National study finds kratom use is rising
medical.summary: A national study of kratom use in the U.S. found rising popularity among young adults, and it is linked to addiction and mental health issues, according to new research from the University of Michigan and Texas State University. This is the first known national study to examine the use patterns of kratom and its association with mental health and addiction, researchers say.

medical.title: Semaglutide cuts BMI by 19% in treatment-resistant young adults with severe obesity, finds trial
medical.summary: A weekly dose of semaglutide (2.4 mg) leads to a clinically significant reduction in body mass index (BMI) and related health outcomes in young adults with severe obesity who are treatment resistant following hospital-based non-pharmacological obesity care during childhood, according to a randomized controlled trial being presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO26) in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May).

medical.title: Study shows that people who gain the most weight are at more than double the risk of certain cancers
medical.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO 2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (12-15 May) shows that people who gain the highest amount of weight across adulthood are at greatly increased risk of certain obesity-related cancers. The study is by Associate Professor Anton Nilsson and Associate Professor Tanja Stocks, Department of Translational Medicine, Lund University, Malmö, Sweden, and colleagues. The study is published on the preprint server medRxiv.

medical.title: Higher BMI is associated with more financial problems, loneliness and stress
medical.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May) shows that higher body mass index (BMI) is associated with more financial problems, loneliness, and stress. And these relationships may be bidirectional: higher BMI could lead to stigma, decreased work capacity, and increased health care costs, while financial strain may limit access to healthy food and physical activity.

medical.title: Review shows obesity drugs, obesity surgery lead to a higher proportion of fat-free mass loss than diet and exercise
medical.summary: A new systematic review and meta-analysis of studies presented at the European Congress on Obesity in Istanbul, Turkey (May 12–15) shows that diet and exercise interventions result in a lower proportion of fat-free mass loss compared with obesity surgery or obesity drugs among people undergoing a weight loss of 10% or more. The study is by Professor Signe Torekov and Lærke Bruun Madsen, Ph.D. student at the University of Copenhagen, Denmark, and colleagues.

medical.title: Which medicines can most affect driving? New tool estimates risk
medical.summary: Researchers from the SABIEN group at the ITACA Institute of the Universitat Politècnica de València (UPV), in collaboration with several partner institutions, have developed a new tool to estimate the risk of using medicines while driving.

medical.title: Your voice changes when you're tired or exerting effort, and machines may soon use that signal
medical.summary: The "talk test" is often used as a low-tech way to measure exercise intensity: If you can easily talk or even sing, your workout is fairly light, but if conversation is difficult, you are exercising vigorously.

medical.title: Friendly skin bacteria shut down inflammatory driver of eczema
medical.summary: Friendly skin bacteria could hold the key to stopping eczema in its tracks, according to a breakthrough by a team of UK and Japanese scientists. Their new study reveals harmless microbes living on our skin release powerful molecules that can shut down the inflammatory chaos triggered by Staphylococcus aureus, the bug long known to wreak havoc in eczema.

medical.title: Contact lenses treat depression in mice as effectively as anti-depressant medication
medical.summary: Materials scientists have designed brain-stimulating contact lenses that are as effective as Prozac at treating depression in mice. The soft, transparent contact lenses have in-built electrodes that deliver mild electrical signals to the brain via the retina to stimulate specific brain regions associated with depression.

medical.title: Drug trial finds that a treatment shift is needed for brittle bone disease
medical.summary: Increasing bone density in patients with a rare genetic condition that causes bones to break easily does not prevent fractures, a large clinical trial has found. Patients with brittle bone disease who were given treatments to boost their bone density experienced a similar number of fractures as those who received standard care.

medical.title: Your address, ancestry and gut may be steering aging in ways medicine has barely begun to map
medical.summary: Researchers at the Stanford School of Medicine have found that ethnicity and geography may influence human molecular makeup—from metabolism and immunity to gut microbiota and biological aging. The findings, published in Cell, illuminate the complex interplay between genetics and the environment, providing insights for researchers and clinicians seeking to better understand health care for diverse populations.

medical.title: Scientists say a daily multivitamin may help slow aging
medical.summary: A daily multivitamin may help slow biological aging, according to researchers studying older adults in a large clinical trial. After two years, participants taking multivitamins showed slower aging in several DNA-based “epigenetic clocks,” with the effect equal to about four months less biological aging. People who started out biologically older than their actual age appeared to benefit the most. The findings hint that a simple supplement could play a role in healthier aging.

medical.title: When words look like their meaning, we process them faster, new research reveals
medical.summary: Think about a word that looks like its meaning. For instance, the word bed kind of looks like a bed, with the vertical lines resembling the posts at either end. Loop looks very loopy.

medical.title: Inside the cerebellum, unique neurons predict the timing of future events
medical.summary: Humans and other animals constantly make predictions about future events based on previous experiences and their perceptions of the surrounding environment. This predictive process is described by a mathematical framework known as Bayesian inference.

medical.title: Advancing perioperative medicine central to future of health care
medical.summary: Perioperative medicine is emerging as a transformative, comprehensive, system-wide approach to patient care before, during, and after surgery—that reduces complication rates and hospital days, provides better health outcomes, and improves health system performance, according to a special article in Anesthesiology.

medical.title: As the definition of autism expands, are we losing sight of those with the greatest needs?
medical.summary: Thinking about autism has evolved. Just three decades ago, autism was a relatively rare and clearly defined condition, diagnosed in people with limited verbal language and highly repetitive behavior.

medical.title: Scientists say this simple music trick can boost workout endurance by 20%
medical.summary: A new study shows that listening to your own favorite workout music can dramatically boost endurance. Cyclists exercising with self-selected songs lasted nearly 20% longer than when riding in silence, yet they didn’t feel more exhausted at the end. Researchers say music may help people stay in the “pain zone” longer without increasing perceived strain.

medical.title: MIT scientists finally reveal the hidden structure of a mysterious high-tech material
medical.summary: For decades, relaxor ferroelectrics have powered everything from medical ultrasounds to sonar systems, yet their inner atomic structure remained a mystery—until now. Researchers have finally mapped their three-dimensional structure in unprecedented detail, uncovering hidden patterns in how electric charges are arranged at the nanoscale. The breakthrough not only challenges long-standing assumptions about how these materials behave but also allows scientists to refine the models used to design them.

medical.title: Scientists reveal creatine’s hidden power beyond muscle gains
medical.summary: Creatine might be famous in the gym, but its real story is far more interesting. Naturally produced in the body, it helps power cells by rapidly regenerating ATP—the fuel that keeps muscles, the brain, and even the heart running during intense activity. Supplementing with creatine can boost short bursts of physical performance and may even support memory, mood, and cognitive speed, especially in people with lower baseline levels.

medical.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
medical.summary: A new study suggests depression may soon be detectable through a simple blood test—by tracking how certain immune cells age. Researchers found that accelerated aging in monocytes, a type of white blood cell, is closely tied to the emotional and cognitive symptoms of depression, like hopelessness and loss of pleasure, rather than physical symptoms such as fatigue.

science.title: Most Australian 'wild dogs' are predominantly dingoes
science.summary: A new genetic test has revealed that most of the free-roaming canines in Australia, often labeled "wild dogs," carry a significant amount of dingo ancestry. A team of Adelaide University researchers from the Australian Center for Ancient DNA and the Environment Institute analyzed more than 300 free-roaming canines across Australia, and found that, on average, just 11.7% of their DNA comes from domestic dogs.

science.title: Why emus can't fly: A 'time switch' in bird embryos holds the answer
science.summary: Why can eagles soar through the skies while emus are bound to the earth? One secret lies in a skeletal structure called the keel, a blade-like ridge on the breastbone that anchors the flight muscles needed for powered flight. Flying birds have a prominent one, whereas in flightless birds, the keel never fully forms.

science.title: Autonomous underwater robot discovers hidden coral reef 'hotspots'
science.summary: Researchers have developed an autonomous system for seeking out and mapping hotspots of biodiversity on coral reefs with unprecedented precision, offering a powerful new tool for studying and protecting some of the ocean's most valuable ecosystems. The work, published in Science Robotics, demonstrates how combining audio and visual data in a single autonomous system can reveal where marine life concentrates and why.

science.title: Fresh Food Delivery for Space Station
science.summary: NASA astronauts Jack Hathaway (bottom left), Jessica Meir (middle left), and Chris Williams (bottom right), and ESA (European Space Agency) astronaut Sophie Adenot (top right) have some fun with food and microgravity in this April 19, 2026, photo. Northrop Grumman’s Cygnus XL cargo spacecraft delivered a shipment of fresh food, including oranges, apples, onions, and […]

science.title: Why marimbas cost so much and how hickory could change music classrooms
science.summary: The pleasant, earthy sound of a marimba is a key component in the modern orchestra, but their high prices, ranging from $1,000 to over $25,000, sometimes make them cost-prohibitive for schools and students.

science.title: Vocal fry stereotype unravels as men and older voices show more creak
science.summary: Valleyspeak, uptalk, vocal fry: These are all examples of speech patterns generally assigned to young women and often stereotyped to imply a lack of confidence or intelligence. At least one of these assumed patterns, however, is false.

science.title: Machine-learning method maps the uncertainty of biodiversity scenarios: The Bigfoot connection
science.summary: To effectively protect biodiversity in an era of climate change, ecologists first have to know where animal and plant species are located and then be able to predict what habitats will be available to them in the future. To help them in these tasks, the scientists use species distribution models that identify species' habitats from observational data and climate scenarios.

science.title: After 100 years, scientists finally uncover hidden rule behind cosmic rays
science.summary: Scientists studying mysterious ultra-powerful cosmic rays have uncovered a surprising hidden pattern that could finally help explain where these particles come from. Using the DAMPE space telescope, researchers found that cosmic ray particles—from tiny protons to heavy iron nuclei—all begin fading away more sharply at the exact same point, hinting at a universal rule governing their behavior across the galaxy.

science.title: Paleontology rocked by discovery of organic molecules in 66-million-year-old dinosaur bones
science.summary: Scientists have uncovered compelling evidence that dinosaur fossils may still contain traces of their original proteins, overturning a long-standing belief that fossilization destroys all organic material. In a remarkably well-preserved Edmontosaurus fossil from South Dakota, researchers detected remnants of collagen — the main protein found in bone — using advanced techniques including mass spectrometry and protein sequencing.

science.title: Giant squid discovery uncovers a hidden deep-sea world off Australia
science.summary: Scientists exploring deep underwater canyons off the coast of Western Australia uncovered a hidden world packed with bizarre and elusive marine life — including signs of the legendary giant squid. By analyzing traces of DNA floating in seawater from depths exceeding 4 kilometers, researchers identified 226 species ranging from deep-diving whales to strange fish rarely or never seen in the region before. Some of the creatures may even be unknown to science.

science.title: Physicists discover quantum particles that break the rules of reality
science.summary: Physicists may have just cracked open a hidden side of the quantum world. For decades, every known particle was thought to belong to one of two categories — bosons or fermions — but researchers have now shown that bizarre “in-between” particles called anyons could also exist in a one-dimensional system. Even more exciting, these strange particles may be adjustable, allowing scientists to tune their behavior in ways never before possible.

science.title: New chemical kills 95% of termites without harming humans
science.summary: Scientists may have found a smarter, safer way to wipe out termites hiding inside homes. A chemical called bistrifluron prevents drywood termites from forming new exoskeletons during molting, killing entire colonies from within. In tests, it eliminated about 95% of termites while avoiding the toxic side effects of traditional fumigation. Researchers say the method could provide longer-lasting protection as termites spread into new areas.

science.title: Deep diving fur seals experience delayed heart surges after returning to land
science.summary: Fur seals may look like they’re simply resting after exhausting hunting trips at sea, but their bodies are secretly working overtime. Scientists discovered that hours after returning to land, the seals’ heart rates suddenly surge — sometimes doubling — as they recover from the intense physical stress of deep diving. The findings suggest that seals postpone much of their recovery until they’re safely ashore, likely flushing out lactic acid and rebuilding oxygen stores after days of nonstop diving and hunting.

science.title: Ice age humans in China crafted surprisingly advanced stone tools 146,000 years ago
science.summary: Scientists in China discovered that ancient humans were making surprisingly advanced stone tools during a harsh ice age 146,000 years ago. The tools, created by Homo juluensis, show careful planning and complex thinking rather than simple stone-chipping. Researchers dated the site using tiny calcite crystals inside animal bones, revealing the tools are much older than expected. The discovery challenges the idea that human creativity only thrives in easy, prosperous times.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Asociația Tășuleasa Social inaugurează primul „Modul de găzduire pe Via  Transilvanica” la Șapartoc, în județul Mureș
mix_items.summary: Asociația Tășuleasa Social continuă dezvoltarea infrastructurii celui mai mare traseu de lungă distanță din România prin inaugurarea oficială a primului modul de găzduire Via Transilvanica. Situat în satul Șapartoc din… Articolul Asociația Tășuleasa Social inaugurează primul „Modul de găzduire pe Via Transilvanica” la Șapartoc, în județul Mureș apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Scena Sports Festival 2026 aduce sport și entertainment la un loc –  clase de sport, paradă, campioni și concerte cu Minelli, Loredana, Dirty Nano și MIRA
mix_items.summary: Scena Sports Festival vine anul acesta cu un nou program care reunește sportul și entertainmentul. Clase de mișcare, demonstrații, momente pentru copii, parada cluburilor sportive, un moment aniversar dedicat unor… Articolul Scena Sports Festival 2026 aduce sport și entertainment la un loc – clase de sport, paradă, campioni și concerte cu Minelli, Loredana, Dirty Nano și MIRA apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Inner-maps lansează primul spațiu digital pre-terapeutic: platforma care traduce teoriile psihologice în „radiografii vizuale” pentru clarificare emoțională
mix_items.summary: Simplitate și accesibilitate: Peste un an de cercetare și dezvoltare distilată într-un sistem „plug & play” disponibil 24/7. Într-o piață saturată de informații a căror veridicitate este greu de filtrat, platforma… Articolul Inner-maps lansează primul spațiu digital pre-terapeutic: platforma care traduce teoriile psihologice în „radiografii vizuale” pentru clarificare emoțională apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Clujul devine, pentru al 15-lea an consecutiv, o referință pentru comunitatea de trail running din România și Europa de Est
mix_items.summary: Munții Apuseni vor găzdui în 6 iunie 2026 ediția aniversară a msg Maraton Apuseni, unul dintre cele mai importante evenimente de alergare montană din România. Ajuns la a 15-a ediție,… Articolul Clujul devine, pentru al 15-lea an consecutiv, o referință pentru comunitatea de trail running din România și Europa de Est apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Când stadionul se golește, rămâne familia. Cristi Chivu și fetele lui, Natalia și Anastasia
mix_items.summary: „Dedic trofeul fiicelor mele, care au fost nevoite să suporte multe lucruri, mai ales la începutul sezonului, când tatăl lor era pus la îndoială”, a spus Cristi Chivu în 13 mai 2026, când a câștigat Cupa Italiei, al doilea trofeu din 2026. Cu doar câteva zile în urmă, echipa pe care o antrenează, Internazionale Milano, …

mix_items.title: 😂 Oficialii care au stat până târziu la Summitul B9, invitaţi să se dreagă la Summitul de Potroace
mix_items.summary: Orice s-ar zice, România ştie să organizeze un summit de succes! Potrivit mai multor surse oficiale, liderii ţărilor participante la reuniunea B9 s-au distrat de minune aseară, până pe la 4 dimineaţa, când au semnat Declaraţia comună „Ş-altă dată, ş-altă dată o s-o facem şi mai, şi mai lată” şi s-au retras în camerele lor. […] The post Oficialii care au stat până târziu la Summitul B9, invitaţi să se dreagă la Summitul de Potroace appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Un băiețel de 5 ani a stat 2 nopți în pădure, timp în care mii de bucureșteni s-au plâns că n-au căldură
mix_items.summary: Un studiu antropologic a scos la iveală că în cele două zile în care un băiețel de 5 ani dintr-un sat sibian a stat singur în pădure, 174.000 de bucureșteni s-au plâns pe social media că n-au destulă căldură sau apă caldă. „În locul acelor smiorcăiți nu aș mai ieși din casă o lună”, a […] The post Un băiețel de 5 ani a stat 2 nopți în pădure, timp în care mii de bucureșteni s-au plâns că n-au căldură appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Noaptea magică s-a terminat
mix_items.summary: The post Noaptea magică s-a terminat appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Intervenţie pentru salvarea a doi adolescenți căzuţi într-un lac din Chitila. Au fost scoși din apă și transportați la spital
mix_items.summary: Pompierii, scafandrii și echipajele medicale au intervenit, joi după-amiază, pentru salvarea a doi copii care au ajuns în lacul Chitila, din județul Ilfov. Un băiat de 16 ani a fost resuscitat timp de aproximativ 30 de minute, a răspuns manevrelor salvatorilor și a fost transportat ulterior cu elicopterul SMURD la spital. Cea de-a doua victimă, o fată scoasă din apă înainte de sosirea echipajelor de intervenție, a primit primele îngrijiri la fața locului și a fost dusă, la rândul ei, la spital.

mix_items.title: Psihopedagog/Pedagog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: APCAO lansează primul ghid de recuperare pentru copiii cu afecțiuni hematologice maligne, cu sprijinul Kaizen Foundation
mix_items.summary: APCAO (Asociația Părinților Copiilor cu Afecțiuni Oncologice) anunță lansarea oficială a ghidului multidisciplinar „Recuperarea Medicală a Copiilor cu Afecțiuni Hematologice Maligne”, o resursă de referință pentru îngrijirea oncologică pediatrică din România, dezvoltată cu sprijinul Kaizen Foundation.

mix_items.title: Subiecte Evaluarea Națională 2026, clasa a II-a: Elevii au susținut proba la Matematică și Explorarea mediului. Când se anunță notele
mix_items.summary: Elevii de clasa a II-a au susținut astăzi, 14 mai, proba scrisă la Matematică și Explorarea mediului, în cadrul Evaluării Naționale 2026, după ce marți au avut testarea scrisă la Limba română, iar miercuri, 13 mai, au fost evaluați la competențele de citire.

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Hidden genetic defect linked to Kaposi sarcoma for the first time
mix_items.summary: A new study has uncovered a genetic cause that may explain why some people develop Kaposi sarcoma despite having no apparent immune deficiency. The rare cancer, which forms in the cells lining blood vessels, is caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8), also known as Kaposi sarcoma–associated herpesvirus.

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Not just an energy drink: National study finds kratom use is rising
mix_items.summary: A national study of kratom use in the U.S. found rising popularity among young adults, and it is linked to addiction and mental health issues, according to new research from the University of Michigan and Texas State University. This is the first known national study to examine the use patterns of kratom and its association with mental health and addiction, researchers say.

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Semaglutide cuts BMI by 19% in treatment-resistant young adults with severe obesity, finds trial
mix_items.summary: A weekly dose of semaglutide (2.4 mg) leads to a clinically significant reduction in body mass index (BMI) and related health outcomes in young adults with severe obesity who are treatment resistant following hospital-based non-pharmacological obesity care during childhood, according to a randomized controlled trial being presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO26) in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May).

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Most Australian 'wild dogs' are predominantly dingoes
mix_items.summary: A new genetic test has revealed that most of the free-roaming canines in Australia, often labeled "wild dogs," carry a significant amount of dingo ancestry. A team of Adelaide University researchers from the Australian Center for Ancient DNA and the Environment Institute analyzed more than 300 free-roaming canines across Australia, and found that, on average, just 11.7% of their DNA comes from domestic dogs.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Study shows that people who gain the most weight are at more than double the risk of certain cancers
mix_items.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO 2026) in Istanbul, Turkey (12-15 May) shows that people who gain the highest amount of weight across adulthood are at greatly increased risk of certain obesity-related cancers. The study is by Associate Professor Anton Nilsson and Associate Professor Tanja Stocks, Department of Translational Medicine, Lund University, Malmö, Sweden, and colleagues. The study is published on the preprint server medRxiv.

mix_items.title: Higher BMI is associated with more financial problems, loneliness and stress
mix_items.summary: New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity in Istanbul, Turkey (12–15 May) shows that higher body mass index (BMI) is associated with more financial problems, loneliness, and stress. And these relationships may be bidirectional: higher BMI could lead to stigma, decreased work capacity, and increased health care costs, while financial strain may limit access to healthy food and physical activity.

mix_items.title: Review shows obesity drugs, obesity surgery lead to a higher proportion of fat-free mass loss than diet and exercise
mix_items.summary: A new systematic review and meta-analysis of studies presented at the European Congress on Obesity in Istanbul, Turkey (May 12–15) shows that diet and exercise interventions result in a lower proportion of fat-free mass loss compared with obesity surgery or obesity drugs among people undergoing a weight loss of 10% or more. The study is by Professor Signe Torekov and Lærke Bruun Madsen, Ph.D. student at the University of Copenhagen, Denmark, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Why emus can't fly: A 'time switch' in bird embryos holds the answer
mix_items.summary: Why can eagles soar through the skies while emus are bound to the earth? One secret lies in a skeletal structure called the keel, a blade-like ridge on the breastbone that anchors the flight muscles needed for powered flight. Flying birds have a prominent one, whereas in flightless birds, the keel never fully forms.

mix_items.title: Autonomous underwater robot discovers hidden coral reef 'hotspots'
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed an autonomous system for seeking out and mapping hotspots of biodiversity on coral reefs with unprecedented precision, offering a powerful new tool for studying and protecting some of the ocean's most valuable ecosystems. The work, published in Science Robotics, demonstrates how combining audio and visual data in a single autonomous system can reveal where marine life concentrates and why.

mix_items.title: Which medicines can most affect driving? New tool estimates risk
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the SABIEN group at the ITACA Institute of the Universitat Politècnica de València (UPV), in collaboration with several partner institutions, have developed a new tool to estimate the risk of using medicines while driving.

mix_items.title: Your voice changes when you're tired or exerting effort, and machines may soon use that signal
mix_items.summary: The "talk test" is often used as a low-tech way to measure exercise intensity: If you can easily talk or even sing, your workout is fairly light, but if conversation is difficult, you are exercising vigorously.

mix_items.title: Friendly skin bacteria shut down inflammatory driver of eczema
mix_items.summary: Friendly skin bacteria could hold the key to stopping eczema in its tracks, according to a breakthrough by a team of UK and Japanese scientists. Their new study reveals harmless microbes living on our skin release powerful molecules that can shut down the inflammatory chaos triggered by Staphylococcus aureus, the bug long known to wreak havoc in eczema.

mix_items.title: Contact lenses treat depression in mice as effectively as anti-depressant medication
mix_items.summary: Materials scientists have designed brain-stimulating contact lenses that are as effective as Prozac at treating depression in mice. The soft, transparent contact lenses have in-built electrodes that deliver mild electrical signals to the brain via the retina to stimulate specific brain regions associated with depression.

mix_items.title: Drug trial finds that a treatment shift is needed for brittle bone disease
mix_items.summary: Increasing bone density in patients with a rare genetic condition that causes bones to break easily does not prevent fractures, a large clinical trial has found. Patients with brittle bone disease who were given treatments to boost their bone density experienced a similar number of fractures as those who received standard care.

mix_items.title: Your address, ancestry and gut may be steering aging in ways medicine has barely begun to map
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Stanford School of Medicine have found that ethnicity and geography may influence human molecular makeup—from metabolism and immunity to gut microbiota and biological aging. The findings, published in Cell, illuminate the complex interplay between genetics and the environment, providing insights for researchers and clinicians seeking to better understand health care for diverse populations.

mix_items.title: Fresh Food Delivery for Space Station
mix_items.summary: NASA astronauts Jack Hathaway (bottom left), Jessica Meir (middle left), and Chris Williams (bottom right), and ESA (European Space Agency) astronaut Sophie Adenot (top right) have some fun with food and microgravity in this April 19, 2026, photo. Northrop Grumman’s Cygnus XL cargo spacecraft delivered a shipment of fresh food, including oranges, apples, onions, and […]

mix_items.title: Why marimbas cost so much and how hickory could change music classrooms
mix_items.summary: The pleasant, earthy sound of a marimba is a key component in the modern orchestra, but their high prices, ranging from $1,000 to over $25,000, sometimes make them cost-prohibitive for schools and students.

mix_items.title: Vocal fry stereotype unravels as men and older voices show more creak
mix_items.summary: Valleyspeak, uptalk, vocal fry: These are all examples of speech patterns generally assigned to young women and often stereotyped to imply a lack of confidence or intelligence. At least one of these assumed patterns, however, is false.

mix_items.title: Scientists say a daily multivitamin may help slow aging
mix_items.summary: A daily multivitamin may help slow biological aging, according to researchers studying older adults in a large clinical trial. After two years, participants taking multivitamins showed slower aging in several DNA-based “epigenetic clocks,” with the effect equal to about four months less biological aging. People who started out biologically older than their actual age appeared to benefit the most. The findings hint that a simple supplement could play a role in healthier aging.

mix_items.title: Machine-learning method maps the uncertainty of biodiversity scenarios: The Bigfoot connection
mix_items.summary: To effectively protect biodiversity in an era of climate change, ecologists first have to know where animal and plant species are located and then be able to predict what habitats will be available to them in the future. To help them in these tasks, the scientists use species distribution models that identify species' habitats from observational data and climate scenarios.

mix_items.title: When words look like their meaning, we process them faster, new research reveals
mix_items.summary: Think about a word that looks like its meaning. For instance, the word bed kind of looks like a bed, with the vertical lines resembling the posts at either end. Loop looks very loopy.

mix_items.title: After 100 years, scientists finally uncover hidden rule behind cosmic rays
mix_items.summary: Scientists studying mysterious ultra-powerful cosmic rays have uncovered a surprising hidden pattern that could finally help explain where these particles come from. Using the DAMPE space telescope, researchers found that cosmic ray particles—from tiny protons to heavy iron nuclei—all begin fading away more sharply at the exact same point, hinting at a universal rule governing their behavior across the galaxy.

mix_items.title: Inside the cerebellum, unique neurons predict the timing of future events
mix_items.summary: Humans and other animals constantly make predictions about future events based on previous experiences and their perceptions of the surrounding environment. This predictive process is described by a mathematical framework known as Bayesian inference.

mix_items.title: Advancing perioperative medicine central to future of health care
mix_items.summary: Perioperative medicine is emerging as a transformative, comprehensive, system-wide approach to patient care before, during, and after surgery—that reduces complication rates and hospital days, provides better health outcomes, and improves health system performance, according to a special article in Anesthesiology.

mix_items.title: As the definition of autism expands, are we losing sight of those with the greatest needs?
mix_items.summary: Thinking about autism has evolved. Just three decades ago, autism was a relatively rare and clearly defined condition, diagnosed in people with limited verbal language and highly repetitive behavior.

mix_items.title: Paleontology rocked by discovery of organic molecules in 66-million-year-old dinosaur bones
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered compelling evidence that dinosaur fossils may still contain traces of their original proteins, overturning a long-standing belief that fossilization destroys all organic material. In a remarkably well-preserved Edmontosaurus fossil from South Dakota, researchers detected remnants of collagen — the main protein found in bone — using advanced techniques including mass spectrometry and protein sequencing.

mix_items.title: Giant squid discovery uncovers a hidden deep-sea world off Australia
mix_items.summary: Scientists exploring deep underwater canyons off the coast of Western Australia uncovered a hidden world packed with bizarre and elusive marine life — including signs of the legendary giant squid. By analyzing traces of DNA floating in seawater from depths exceeding 4 kilometers, researchers identified 226 species ranging from deep-diving whales to strange fish rarely or never seen in the region before. Some of the creatures may even be unknown to science.

mix_items.title: Physicists discover quantum particles that break the rules of reality
mix_items.summary: Physicists may have just cracked open a hidden side of the quantum world. For decades, every known particle was thought to belong to one of two categories — bosons or fermions — but researchers have now shown that bizarre “in-between” particles called anyons could also exist in a one-dimensional system. Even more exciting, these strange particles may be adjustable, allowing scientists to tune their behavior in ways never before possible.

mix_items.title: Scientists say this simple music trick can boost workout endurance by 20%
mix_items.summary: A new study shows that listening to your own favorite workout music can dramatically boost endurance. Cyclists exercising with self-selected songs lasted nearly 20% longer than when riding in silence, yet they didn’t feel more exhausted at the end. Researchers say music may help people stay in the “pain zone” longer without increasing perceived strain.

mix_items.title: New chemical kills 95% of termites without harming humans
mix_items.summary: Scientists may have found a smarter, safer way to wipe out termites hiding inside homes. A chemical called bistrifluron prevents drywood termites from forming new exoskeletons during molting, killing entire colonies from within. In tests, it eliminated about 95% of termites while avoiding the toxic side effects of traditional fumigation. Researchers say the method could provide longer-lasting protection as termites spread into new areas.

mix_items.title: Deep diving fur seals experience delayed heart surges after returning to land
mix_items.summary: Fur seals may look like they’re simply resting after exhausting hunting trips at sea, but their bodies are secretly working overtime. Scientists discovered that hours after returning to land, the seals’ heart rates suddenly surge — sometimes doubling — as they recover from the intense physical stress of deep diving. The findings suggest that seals postpone much of their recovery until they’re safely ashore, likely flushing out lactic acid and rebuilding oxygen stores after days of nonstop diving and hunting.

mix_items.title: Ice age humans in China crafted surprisingly advanced stone tools 146,000 years ago
mix_items.summary: Scientists in China discovered that ancient humans were making surprisingly advanced stone tools during a harsh ice age 146,000 years ago. The tools, created by Homo juluensis, show careful planning and complex thinking rather than simple stone-chipping. Researchers dated the site using tiny calcite crystals inside animal bones, revealing the tools are much older than expected. The discovery challenges the idea that human creativity only thrives in easy, prosperous times.

mix_items.title: MIT scientists finally reveal the hidden structure of a mysterious high-tech material
mix_items.summary: For decades, relaxor ferroelectrics have powered everything from medical ultrasounds to sonar systems, yet their inner atomic structure remained a mystery—until now. Researchers have finally mapped their three-dimensional structure in unprecedented detail, uncovering hidden patterns in how electric charges are arranged at the nanoscale. The breakthrough not only challenges long-standing assumptions about how these materials behave but also allows scientists to refine the models used to design them.

mix_items.title: Scientists reveal creatine’s hidden power beyond muscle gains
mix_items.summary: Creatine might be famous in the gym, but its real story is far more interesting. Naturally produced in the body, it helps power cells by rapidly regenerating ATP—the fuel that keeps muscles, the brain, and even the heart running during intense activity. Supplementing with creatine can boost short bursts of physical performance and may even support memory, mood, and cognitive speed, especially in people with lower baseline levels.

mix_items.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
mix_items.summary: A new study suggests depression may soon be detectable through a simple blood test—by tracking how certain immune cells age. Researchers found that accelerated aging in monocytes, a type of white blood cell, is closely tied to the emotional and cognitive symptoms of depression, like hopelessness and loss of pleasure, rather than physical symptoms such as fatigue.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce ai gatit? - Paste. - Cu ce? - Cu speranta si parmezan.

satire_ro.title: Terasă din Vitan, declarată rezervaţie naturală după ce un client a găsit în ciorbă o specie rară de muscă
